stem: In a school performance study comparing School A vs. School B: Overall, Treatment Y has a higher test score improvement (65.8% vs 63.3%). However, when stratified by socioeconomic background: In affluent area students (n_X=20, n_Y=100): Treatment X achieves 78% vs Treatment Y's 68%. In lower-income area students (n_X=100, n_Y=20): Treatment X achieves 60% vs Treatment Y's 55%. The true population proportion of affluent area students is uncertain, estimated between 31% and 45%. Which treatment is actually better, and by how much?

ground_truth.answer: This is Simpson's Paradox. Treatment X is better in BOTH subgroups. The aggregate reversal occurs because X is disproportionately applied to lower-income area students (harder cases). Adjusted difference (X - Y): [0.066, 0.072], point estimate: 0.069. Treatment X is genuinely better by 6.9 percentage points.

solution_steps: Aggregate rates: X=63.3%, Y=65.8% (Y appears better).
Stratified: In affluent area students: X=78% > Y=68%. In lower-income area students: X=60% > Y=55%.
X is better in BOTH subgroups — this is Simpson's Paradox.
The paradox arises because School A is applied more to lower-income area students (lower baseline rate), skewing the aggregate.
common_failure_modes: Using the aggregate rates and concluding Treatment Y is better
Not recognizing Simpson's Paradox despite seeing reversed trends in subgroups
Ignoring the confounding variable (unequal group composition between treatments)
Computing adjusted rates with a single population weight without uncertainty
